Title: Artificial intelligence models for prediction of monthly rainfall without climatic data for meteorological stations in Ethiopia

Title: Precipitation nowcasting using deep learning techniques

Title: Deep Learning is blowing in the wind. Deep models applied to wind prediction at turbine level
Meteorological Variable(s): ATMOSPHERIC STATE VARIABLES, DYNAMIC AND KINEMATIC VARIABLES
Model(s) Used: Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) seq2seq, MLP Direct, Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) seq2seq, Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) seq2seq, RNN encoder-decoder, k-Nearest Neighbors (k-NN) (baseline), Persistence (baseline).
Dataset /Input Type: Ground Stations, Satellite/Remote Sensing, Reanalysis, Radar, IoT Sensors, Crowdsourced, CURRATED DATABASES AND BENCHMARK DATASETS, Historical Weather Data, Multiple/Derived
Data Source: U.S. National Renewable Energy Laboratory (NREL) Wind Toolkit.
Sample Size / Time Span/Spatial Coverage: 126,692 wind sites / 7 years (2007–2013)
Key Findings: - Deep learning models outperformed statistical baselines (k-NN, Persistence). - MLP seq2seq had better accuracy than MLP direct regression. - MLP seq2seq showed slightly better average accuracy than RNN and CNN, but differences were narrow. - The best-performing model varied by site, suggesting a dependence on local terrain and wind characteristics (e.g., RNN better in high-variability areas like the Plains and West Coast). - Models showed accuracy differences between test and validation years, indicating that wind regimes change over time, requiring retraining.
Limitation Category: Temporal & Extreme Event Limitations, Data Quality & Methodological Limitations, Forecast Horizon & Operational Limitations
CHALLENGES REPORTED: Data-related challenges, Technical/Methodological challenges, Domain-specific challenges

Title: Deep learning for near-surface air temperature estimation from FengYun 4A satellite data
Meteorological Variable(s): ATMOSPHERIC STATE VARIABLES
Model(s) Used: Transformer-based Neural Network (TaNet) - based on UCTransNet architecture; Compared with U-Net, CFSv2, CRA, ERA5.
Dataset /Input Type: Ground Stations, Satellite/Remote Sensing, Reanalysis, CURRATED DATABASES AND BENCHMARK DATASETS, Historical Weather Data, Multiple/Derived
Data Source: FY-4A/AGRI satellite (China Meteorological Administration), ERA5 reanalysis (ECMWF), CRA reanalysis (CMA), CFSv2 reanalysis (NCEP), Meteorological station observations (CMA).
Sample Size / Time Span/Spatial Coverage: Training: April 2021-May 2022 (14 months); Testing: Jan, Apr, Jul, Oct 2020 (4 months); Station data: 2,231 stations.,Region 50°S-50°N, 40°E-140°E (covering Asia, Oceania, parts of Africa/Europe); 0.25°×0.25° spatial resolution.
Key Findings: - TaNet achieved excellent performance: CC=0.990 with ERA5, CC=0.959 with station observations. - TaNet outperformed CFSv2, CRA, and U-Net across all metrics. - Against ERA5 benchmark: TaNet reduced RMSE by 20.94% vs CFSv2 (1.503°C vs 1.901°C) and 16.92% vs U-Net (1.503°C vs 1.809°C). - Against station observations: TaNet reduced RMSE by 10.55% vs CFSv2 (2.594°C vs 2.900°C), 2.26% vs CRA (2.594°C vs 2.654°C), and 5.54% vs U-Net (2.594°C vs 2.746°C). - TaNet showed particular improvement in challenging regions (mid-high latitudes, Qinghai-Tibet Plateau) and during July (summer conditions).
Limitation Category: Spatial & Geographic Constraints, Temporal & Extreme Event Limitations, Data Quality & Methodological Limitations
CHALLENGES REPORTED: Data-related challenges, Technical/Methodological challenges, Socio-technical challenges, Domain-specific challenges

Title: FuXi: A cascade machine learning forecasting system for 15-day global weather forecast
Meteorological Variable(s): ATMOSPHERIC STATE VARIABLES, DYNAMIC AND KINEMATIC VARIABLES, HYDROLOGICAL AND WATER CYCLE VARIABLES
